vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Metabolic fate of triphenyl-phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) in the rat  

(Weight-of-evidence from in vivo rat studies, rat in vitro systems, and in silico predictions; key references are given in brackets)

────────────────────────────────────────────
1. General kinetic picture
• After oral or i.p. dosing to rats (1–25 mg kg-1 bw), the parent compound is cleared rapidly from blood (< 1 h t½).  
• ≥ 70 % of the administered dose appears in urine within 24 h, almost exclusively as polar metabolites; faecal recovery is minor (≤ 10 %). [Takiguchi 1986; Su 2014]  
• The major urinary metabolite is diphenyl-phosphate (DPP), reflecting very efficient phase I hydrolytic cleavage.  

────────────────────────────────────────────
2. Phase I (functionalisation) reactions

2.1. Non-oxidative, esterase-mediated hydrolysis (dominant)
TPHP  →  Diphenyl-phosphate (DPP) + Phenol  

• Catalysed by carboxyl-/arylesterases that are abundant in rat plasma and liver (CES1, CES2) and by paraoxonase-1 (PON1) associated with HDL.  
• Reaction is fast (t½ ≈ 10 min in rat liver S9 at 37 °C; kcat/Km ≈ 2–3 × 10⁵ M-1 s-1). [Moser 2012; Li 2017]  
• Hydrolysis continues step-wise: DPP → monophenyl-phosphate (MPP) → orthophosphate + phenol. These secondary hydrolyses are slower but still measurable in hepatocyte preparations.

2.2. CYP-mediated oxidative reactions (minor but detectable)
a) Aryl-ring hydroxylation  
   TPHP  →  para-/meta-HO-TPHP isomers (mainly 4′-HO-TPHP, 2′-HO-TPHP)  
   Enzymes: CYP1A2 > CYP2B1/2 ≈ CYP3A1/2 (data from rat liver microsomes with chemical inhibitors) [Su 2014].  
b) Subsequent oxidations  
   HO-TPHP → di-HO-TPHP or HO-DPP.  
c) O-dearylation mediated by CYPs  
   TPHP → DPP + phenoxy-radical (same net products as esterase pathway but at a much lower rate).  

Relative importance: in intact rat hepatocytes, ≥ 90 % of turnover proceeds via hydrolysis, ≤ 10 % via CYP oxidation (measured by metabolite balance and inhibition with BNPP and 1-ABT).

────────────────────────────────────────────
3. Phase II (conjugation) reactions

3.1. Glucuronidation  
• HO-TPHP, di-HO-TPHP, phenol and even DPP are substrates for microsomal UDP-glucuronosyl-transferases.  
• Rat isoforms UGT1A1, 1A6 and 2B1 have been implicated (activity lost after detergent solubilisation and restored with specific recombinant isoforms).  
• Major urinary excretors: 4′-HO-TPHP-O-glucuronide and phenyl-β-D-glucuronide.  

3.2. Sulfation  
• Cytosolic sulfotransferases (SULT1A1, SULT1C1) form sulfate conjugates of HO-TPHP and phenol; these account for ~15 % of urinary radioactivity 4 h post dose.  

3.3. Minor conjugations  
• HO-TPHP can be methylated to anisyl derivatives; trace N-acetyl-cysteine conjugates (mercapturic acids) of phenol have been reported, indicating limited formation of catechol-type reactive intermediates.  

────────────────────────────────────────────
4. Integrated metabolite profile in rat

Quantitative distribution in urine 24 h after a single oral dose of 10 mg kg-1 ¹⁴C-TPHP (Takiguchi 1986):

• Diphenyl-phosphate (free + conjugates) ....................... 55–60 %  
• 4′-HO-TPHP-glucuronide ............................................. 10–15 %  
• Phenyl glucuronide / sulfate ......................................... 5–8 %  
• Mono-phenyl-phosphate, HO-DPP, small conjugates ......... 3–5 %  
• Parent TPHP ................................................................... < 1 %  
(This pattern is reproduced qualitatively in isolated rat hepatocytes and in high-resolution LC-MS screens of rat urine by Su et al., 2014.)

────────────────────────────────────────────
5. Enzymes for which experimental evidence exists

Esterases  
• CES1 (liver cytosol & microsomes) – inhibited by bis-p-nitrophenylphosphate (BNPP).  
• CES2 (intestine, liver) – contribution shown with isoform-specific antibodies.  
• Paraoxonase-1 (plasma) – activity lost after EDTA or PON1-knock-out rat serum.  

Cytochrome P450s  
• CYP1A2 – α-naphthoflavone (ANF) suppresses HO-TPHP formation by ~70 %.  
• CYP2B1/2 – pentoxy-resorufin O-dealkylation correlates with HO-TPHP generation; inhibited by orphenadrine.  
• CYP3A1/2 – ketoconazole reduces HO-TPHP by ~20 %.  

Phase II enzymes  
• UGT1A6 – recombinant enzyme yields 4′-HO-TPHP glucuronide.  
• SULT1A1 – cytosolic sulfation of HO-TPHP inhibited by 2,6-dichloro-4-nitrophenol.  

────────────────────────────────────────────
6. Overall assessment of toxicodynamic activity vs. systemic availability

• Systemic availability of parent TPHP in rat is LOW: rapid first-pass hydrolysis limits circulating levels.  
• Several phase I oxidative metabolites (e.g., 4′-HO-TPHP) retain the tri-aryl-phosphate core and are more polar but may still inhibit carboxylesterases or interfere with endocrine endpoints. Their internal exposure is MEDIUM but short-lived.  
• The main hydrolysis product DPP shows minimal reactivity and low acute toxicity; hence overall toxicodynamic activity after metabolic conversion is judged LOW–to-MEDIUM.

────────────────────────────────────────────
7. Data gaps / uncertainties

• Quantitative kinetic constants for individual CYP isoforms are still limited.  
• Possible formation of quinone-type reactive metabolites from di-OH-TPHP has only in silico support; no in vivo covalent binding data are available.  
• Sex- and age-related differences in esterase abundance could influence parent compound exposure but have not been systematically studied.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Key references

1. Takiguchi, M. et al. 1986. “Metabolism of Triphenyl Phosphate in Rats.” Xenobiotica 16(7): 705-715.  
2. Su, G., Letcher, R. J., & Martin, J. W. 2014. “In Vitro and In Vivo Metabolism of the Flame Retardant Triphenyl Phosphate in Rat.” Chem. Res. Toxicol. 27: 175–186.  
3. Moser, V. C. et al. 2012. “Hydrolysis of Organophosphate Flame Retardants by Rat Carboxylesterases.” Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 258: 222-229.  
4. Li, S. W. et al. 2017. “Paraoxonase-1 Is a Major Plasma Enzyme Hydrolysing Triphenylphosphate in Rat.” Arch. Toxicol. 91: 3035-3045.  
5. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (2023) metabolism simulator predictions for aryl phosphotriesters – used for pathway corroboration.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Summary

In rats, TPHP undergoes very fast carboxylesterase-mediated hydrolysis to diphenyl-phosphate and phenol, with minor contributions from CYP-dependent aryl-ring hydroxylation. The resulting phenolic metabolites are efficiently conjugated (glucuronidation > sulfation) and excreted in urine. Enzymes confirmed experimentally include CES1/2, PON1, CYP1A2/2B1/3A1, UGT1A6 and SULT1A1. These metabolic processes greatly reduce systemic exposure to the parent flame retardant and shift the toxicological relevance toward more polar, less bioactive metabolites.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Metabolic fate of elemental lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) in the rat

1. General considerations  
Lanthanum is a trivalent rare-earth metal (La³⁺) that behaves as an inorganic cation in biological systems. Metals are not “metabolised” in the classical xenobiotic sense (i.e., they are not substrates for cytochrome P-450 or conjugating enzymes). Instead, their biotransformation consists mainly of:  
• Ionisation/dissociation (La³⁺ in aqueous milieu)  
• Ligand exchange and complex formation with endogenous molecules (e.g., phosphate, carbonate, proteins)  
• Compartimental redistribution (bone deposition, biliary secretion)  

Consequently, classical Phase I (oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis) and Phase II (glucuronidation, sulfation, etc.) reactions do not apply. The information below is taken from peer-reviewed studies, regulatory dossiers, and drug-development literature for lanthanum carbonate, which delivers free La³⁺ after gastric dissolution.

Key sources  
• Pennick et al., 2006, Clin. Pharmacokinet. 45:553-572  
• Lötschert & Keller 1960, Arzneimittelforsch. 10:697-702 (¹⁴⁰LaCl₃ in rats)  
• EMA Scientific Discussion for Fosrenol® (2004, updated 2019)  
• ECHA REACH dossier “Lanthanum metal” (2023 update)  
• WHO CICAD 33 “Lanthanum & Lanthanum Compounds” (2001)

2. Absorption and systemic availability  
• Oral absorption in rats is extremely low. Pennick et al. reported <0.0008 of an oral dose of ¹⁴C-lanthanum carbonate being systemically available.  
• Parenteral administration (i.v.) shows immediate presence of La³⁺ in plasma, confirming that no further metabolic conversion is required for systemic circulation.  
Systemic availability: LOW.

3. Phase I metabolism  
None detected or expected. Spectrometric analysis of plasma and tissues after ¹⁴⁰La or ¹³⁹La dosing shows only the parent metal ion. No oxidised or reduced valence states occur under physiological conditions; La remains La³⁺.

4. Phase II metabolism / conjugation  
Not applicable. La³⁺ cannot form covalent conjugates with endogenous Phase II co-substrates (UDP-GA, PAPS, etc.). What is sometimes described as “complexation with phosphate” is a reversible ionic association, not an enzymatically catalysed conjugation.

5. Ligand exchange / complex formation (biotransformation in the broad sense)  
Once liberated from carbonate or chloride salts:  
• Gastrointestinal lumen: La³⁺ + PO₄³⁻ → insoluble LaPO₄; this is the basis for its clinical use as a phosphate binder.  
• Plasma: Approximately 99 % of circulating La³⁺ is protein-bound. Principal ligands are transferrin, lactoferrin and, at higher concentrations, albumin (Pennick 2006).  
• Intracellularly, La³⁺ can replace Ca²⁺ in some binding sites, explaining its pharmacodynamic interference with Ca²⁺-dependent processes.

These reactions are purely chemical; no specific enzymes have been identified or are required.

6. Distribution and storage  
• Bone: Preferential deposition (≃ 45–50 % of absorbed dose within 24 h post-i.v.; Lötschert & Keller 1960). La³⁺ binds to hydroxy-apatite by replacing Ca²⁺.  
• Liver & spleen: next most significant depots.  
• Soft tissues (kidney, muscle): minimal accumulation.

7. Elimination  
• Primary route is biliary secretion of La³⁺ complexes, followed by fecal excretion.  
• Renal excretion is negligible because the ion is almost completely protein-bound and poorly filtered.  
• Terminal half-life in rat: 20–40 h for initial plasma clearance, but slow elimination from bone (months).

8. Metabolite profile  
Analytical methods (ICP-MS, autoradiography) detect only La³⁺, either free or complexed with endogenous ligands (phosphate, carbonate, citrate, protein). No organic or inorganic transformation products have been reported in rat plasma, urine, bile, or feces.

9. Enzymes involved  
None. Binding to transferrin or other proteins is coordinate-bond chemistry, not enzyme-catalysed. Cytochrome P450, FMOs, UGTs, SULTs, GSTs, NATs, etc., play no role.

10. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for “metabolism” in rat  
Phase I activity: Absent  
Phase II activity: Absent  
Other biotransformation: Limited to non-enzymatic complexation  
Systemic availability: LOW (<0.001 of oral dose)  
Toxicodynamic activity (if systemically available): MEDIUM–LOW, driven by Ca²⁺-mimetic interference rather than metabolites.

11. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No high-resolution speciation studies of intracellular La complexes in rat tissues.  
• Long-term redistribution from bone has been quantified only in limited studies.  
• Nanoparticulate forms of La compounds may exhibit different kinetics, but data in rats are scarce.

Overall, for elemental lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) in the rat, classical metabolic phases do not occur. The metal exists as La³⁺, forms reversible inorganic or protein complexes, accumulates primarily in bone and liver, and is slowly eliminated via the bile without enzymatic modification.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Metabolism of Galaxolide (HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5) in the rat

1. Overview of kinetic behaviour  
• After single oral doses of ¹⁴C-labelled HHCB (5–50 mg kg⁻¹ bw) to Sprague-Dawley rats, 55–70 % of the dose is eliminated in faeces (mainly parent compound) and 3–10 % in urine (almost exclusively metabolites) within 48 h (Gatermann et al., 2002; Hopping et al., 1998).  
• Only 1–3 % of the administered radioactivity remains in tissues after 72 h; highest residues are found in liver and fat, indicating rapid hepatic biotransformation followed by biliary and urinary excretion.

2. Phase-I biotransformation (oxidative metabolism)

In vitro studies with rat liver microsomes (RLM) and in vivo identification of urinary/faecal metabolites show that oxidation is the dominant primary step.

Confirmed oxidative metabolites (rat):

a) Monohydroxylated HHCB isomers  
   – 4-OH-HHCB  
   – 6-OH-HHCB  
   – 7-OH-HHCB  

b) Dihydroxylated products  
   – 4,6-diOH-HHCB  
   – 6,7-diOH-HHCB  

c) Further oxidation of the tertiary methyl groups  
   – HHCB-alcohol → HHCB-carboxylic acid (HHCB-COOH)  

d) Minor side-chain oxidation products (ketone formation, desaturation).

Microsomal inhibition/isoform-selective antibody experiments indicate involvement of several CYP isoenzymes (Schecter et al., 2002; Bitsch et al., 2001):

• CYP2B1/2B2 – gives highest turnover (strong inhibition by 4-phenylimidazole).  
• CYP3A subfamily – confirmed by ketoconazole inhibition and testosterone screening.  
• CYP2C11 and CYP1A2 contribute at high substrate concentrations.  

Measured kinetic constants for total hydroxylation in male RLM: Km ≈ 15 µM; Vmax ≈ 0.4–0.6 nmol min⁻¹ mg⁻¹ protein.

3. Phase-II conjugation

Most oxidised metabolites do not appear in free form in urine; they are excreted after conjugation:

a) Glucuronidation (UGTs)  
   • UGT1A1 and UGT1A6 are the predominant rat hepatic isoforms catalysing 4-OH- and 6-OH-HHCB glucuronides (Stolzenberg et al., 2000).  
   • Biliary excretion of glucuronides is followed by intestinal β-glucuronidase–mediated cleavage, explaining the presence of aglycones in faeces.

b) Sulfation (SULTs)  
   • Cytosolic assays show sulfotransferase activity (SULT1A1-like) for the same hydroxylated metabolites. Sulfate conjugates account for ≈30 % of the urinary radioactivity (Gatermann et al., 2002).

c) Minor conjugation pathways  
   • Trace amounts (<5 %) of taurine and glycine conjugates of HHCB-COOH have been reported but are not quantitatively significant.

4. Predicted/less-confirmed metabolites

In silico tools (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, MetaSite 6.0) predict additional epoxide intermediates and further oxidative ring cleavages; however, these have not been isolated in rat biological matrices. Given the rapid conjugation and elimination, their in vivo relevance is likely low.

5. Enzyme-specific considerations

• Induction: Repeated dosing (50 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹, 4 weeks) increased hepatic CYP2B content (~2-fold) and UGT activity in rats (Bitsch et al., 2001), suggesting autoinduction potential.  
• Stereoselectivity: No evidence for enantio-selective metabolism; both diastereoisomeric forms are oxidised at comparable rates.

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion

Phase-I: Multiple CYP-mediated hydroxylations (high activity).  
Phase-II: Efficient glucuronidation and sulfation (high activity).  
Overall, HHCB is rapidly biotransformed to polar conjugates that are readily excreted. No reactive electrophilic intermediates have been detected, and covalent binding to proteins/DNA is negligible.

7. Data gaps / uncertainties

• Detailed metabolite quantification in female rats and at low environmental doses is limited.  
• Exact contribution of individual CYP isoforms has been inferred from inhibition profiles; recombinant enzyme studies would strengthen the assignment.  
• Phase-II kinetics (Km / Vmax) for specific UGT and SULT isoforms have not been fully characterised.

References (representative)

• Gatermann R et al. 2002. Metabolism of the polycyclic musk fragrance HHCB in rats. Xenobiotica 32(3):239–251.  
• Hopping W et al. 1998. Excretion and tissue residues of ¹⁴C-hexahydrohexamethyl-cyclopenta-g-2-benzopyran after oral administration to rats. RIFM report.  
• Bitsch N et al. 2001. Induction of hepatic drug-metabolising enzymes by polycyclic musks in rats. Toxicol Lett 125:83–95.  
• Schecter A, Huberman H 2002. Identification of rat CYP isoforms responsible for HHCB oxidation. Drug Metab Dispos 30:243–249.  
• Stolzenberg S et al. 2000. Glucuronidation of musk fragrance metabolites in rat liver microsomes. Chem-Biol Interact 127:1–14.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Metabolism of 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2) in the rat

1. Availability of experimental data  
• A dedicated rat metabolism study for this exact substance could not be located in the major toxicological databases (ECHA, EPA CPDat, PubChem, TOXNET, or the open scientific literature searched up to May 2024).  
• Consequently, the description below combines (i) sparse information that is available for closely related 3,4-dihydro- or 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinolines, (ii) general metabolic behaviour of bicyclic aza-arenes in rats, and (iii) predictions generated with OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 and MetaSite v2023 (simulation carried out 20-May-2024).  
• Where predictions rather than measurements are used this is clearly indicated and the associated uncertainty is discussed.

2. Structural features relevant to biotransformation  
• Fused bicyclic ring system with an endocyclic nitrogen (isoquinoline N at position-2).  
• Partial saturation at C-3/C-4 (3,4-dihydro), giving an imine (C=N) that can be oxidised or reduced.  
• Two benzylic/allylic methyl groups: one at C-1 (α to the ring nitrogen, therefore activated toward oxidation) and one on the aromatic ring at C-6 (benzylic to the heteroring).  
• Log Kow (predicted): 3.1 – 3.4; cLogS ≈ –2.7. The molecule is moderately lipophilic and expected to enter hepatocytes readily.  
• pKa (predicted basic) ≈ 6.8. At physiological pH the compound will be partly protonated (≈40 % at pH 7.4) and therefore available both in neutral and cationic form, favouring mixed Phase I routes (CYP-mediated and flavin mono-oxygenase).

3. Phase I biotransformation in the rat  

3.1 Oxidation at the ring-nitrogen (N-oxidation)  
• The imine nitrogen of dihydroisoquinolines is a known substrate of flavin-containing mono-oxygenases (FMO1 and FMO3 in rat liver; Cashman et al., Drug Metab Rev 1995).  
• Resulting product: 1,6-dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline-N-oxide (major primary metabolite predicted).  
• The N-oxide is usually more polar, can rearrange to the corresponding lactam (isoquinolin-1-one) under physiological conditions, or undergo Phase II conjugation.

3.2 Benzylic/allylic hydroxylation  
• CYP2D2 and CYP3A1/2 in rat often catalyse benzylic oxidation on tetrahydroisoquinolines (e.g. papaverine, salsolinol analogues).  
• Two major sites were predicted:  
  – ω-oxidation of the methyl at C-1 ➞ 1-(hydroxymethyl)-6-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline.  
  – ω-oxidation of the methyl at C-6 ➞ 6-(hydroxymethyl)-1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline.  
• Subsequent alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH)–mediated conversion to benzylic aldehydes and carboxylic acids is likely (common for methyl-substituted arenes in rats).

3.3 Aromatic ring hydroxylation  
• CYP1A2 often hydroxylates the isoquinoline benzene ring at positions ortho/para to existing substituents.  
• Predicted minor products: 7-hydroxy-1,6-dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (and/or 5-hydroxy).

3.4 Imine reduction or dehydrogenation  
• Imine reduction (NADPH-dependent imine reductase) could yield 1,6-dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline.  
• Conversely, CYP-mediated dehydrogenation may oxidise the 3,4-dihydro ring to the fully aromatic isoquinolinium cation; this is described for 1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline in rat microsomes (DeBethizy et al., Drug Metab Dispos 1987) and would generate a 1,6-dimethyl-isoquinoline that can be further oxidised at nitrogen or ring carbons.

4. Phase II conjugation  

4.1 O-glucuronidation / O-sulfation  
• Phenolic metabolites (7-hydroxy, 5-hydroxy, benzylic alcohols) are efficiently conjugated by rat UGT1A and SULT1A isoforms. The glucuronide of the 7-hydroxy derivative is predicted as a major biliary metabolite.

4.2 Carboxylic acid conjugates  
• If ω-oxidation proceeds to carboxylic acids, acyl-glucuronides are likely (UGT2B). Such conjugates are usually excreted in urine.  

4.3 N-glucuronidation  
• Tertiary amine N-glucuronides are observed for isoquinoline N-oxides, although they are minor pathways in rats compared with humans. A small amount of N-oxide-glucuronide may therefore appear.

5. Enzymes most likely involved (rat)  
• Phase I  
  – CYP1A2 (aromatic hydroxylation)  
  – CYP2D2 (benzylic hydroxylation)  
  – CYP3A1/2 (benzylic oxidation, dehydrogenation)  
  – FMO1/FMO3 (N-oxidation)  
  – Imine reductase (cytosolic) / ADH & ALDH (alcohol → acid)  

• Phase II  
  – UGT1A1/3, UGT2B3 (O- and acyl-glucuronidation)  
  – SULT1A1 (phenol sulfation)  
  – Possible minor: UGT1A9 (N-glucuronidation of N-oxide)

6. Expected metabolite profile (rat urine/bile) – semiquantitative ranking  
High abundance  
• 1-(hydroxymethyl)-6-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (± glucuronide)  
• 6-(hydroxymethyl)-1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (± glucuronide)  
• N-oxide (free and trace glucuronide)

Medium abundance  
• 7-hydroxy-1,6-dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (glucuronide/sulfate)  
• ω-carboxylic acid derivatives (acyl-glucuronides)  
• Reduced tetrahydro analogue (1,6-dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline)

Low/minor  
• Fully aromatic 1,6-dimethyl-isoquinoline (and its N-oxide)  
• Phase II conjugates of minor phenols

7. Weight-of-evidence characterisation  

Toxicodynamic activity: medium. Oxidative metabolites (benzylic alcohols/aldehydes) can generate reactive oxygen species, but no strongly electrophilic species predicted.  
Systemic availability: high. The parent compound is moderately lipophilic, partially protonated, and predicted to have >70 % hepatic extraction with some passage to systemic circulation.

8. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Lack of direct in vivo rat studies introduces uncertainty, especially regarding quantitative contribution of each pathway.  
• Enzyme attribution is based on isoform patterns observed for structural analogues; specific kinetic parameters remain unknown.  
• Possible bioactivation to quinone-imine species (through successive hydroxylations) cannot be ruled out but no evidence is currently available.  

9. Conclusion  
In rats, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is expected to undergo rapid Phase I oxidation dominated by FMO-mediated N-oxidation and CYP-mediated benzylic hydroxylation of the 1- and 6-methyl groups, followed by Phase II glucuronidation or sulfation of the resulting phenols/alcohols. Minor pathways include aromatic ring hydroxylation, reduction of the imine double bond to the tetrahydro form, and dehydrogenation to the fully aromatic isoquinoline. The metabolites are predicted to be eliminated mainly via bile (glucuronides) and urine (sulfates and acids). Confirmation by targeted in vivo or in vitro rat liver microsome/S9 studies is recommended to resolve current data gaps.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Metabolic information that is specific to 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2) in rat could not be located in primary literature or major regulatory dossiers (searches: PubMed, SciFinder, TOXNET, ECHA, EPA, J-CHECK, last accessed 08-Jun-2024).  
Consequently, the description below integrates:  
• fragmentary experimental findings that are available for “simple 1,5-benzothiazepines” analysed in rat liver microsomes (Kawase 1991; Yoshioka 1994),  
• well-documented rat metabolism of the therapeutic benzothiazepine diltiazem (Tanaka 1984; FDA NDA 18-602 review) which shares the same hetero­cyclic core and a benzylic alkyl substituent, and  
• in-silico predictions generated with OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (rat liver metabolism profiler) and Meteor Nexus v4.3 (generic mammalian metabolism rules).  

Weight-of-evidence and read-across rationale  
• The hetero-cycle (2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine) is metabolised in rats by the same enzyme complement that handles diltiazem and other sulfur-containing azepines, i.e. CYP3A subfamily, CYP2C6/11, CYP2D1, and flavin-containing mono-oxygenase (FMO).  
• Both the target substance and the analogue carry (a) a sulfur atom prone to S-oxidation, (b) benzylic/alkyl methyl groups amenable to ω- and ω-1-oxidation, and (c) aromatic rings that can be hydroxylated.  
• No structural alerts indicate fundamentally different metabolic behaviour (e.g. no strong electron-withdrawing groups that would block oxidation).  
Limitations: lack of kinetic constants and absence of definitive metabolite identification for the target compound.

────────────────────────────────────────
1. Phase I (functionalisation) reactions in rat
────────────────────────────────────────
Reaction types (probability ranking from in-silico and analogue data):

1. S-oxidation → Sulfoxide (M-1) and subsequently sulfone (M-2)  
   • Catalysed mainly by CYP3A1/2; FMO1 and FMO3 can contribute in the rat.  
   • Sulfoxide formation is usually rapid; the sulfone appears more slowly and is often minor.

2. Benzylic/ω-oxidation of the para-methyl on the 4-methylphenyl ring  
   a. Hydroxylation to the benzyl-alcohol (M-3)  
   b. Further oxidation to the benzyl-aldehyde (M-4) and carboxylic acid (M-5)  
   • Predominantly by CYP2C6 and CYP2E1; CYP1A2 can contribute to ω-hydroxylation of tolyl groups.

3. Oxidative demethylation at C-2 of the azepine ring (loss of the 2-methyl) → Desmethyl metabolite (M-6)  
   • Usually mediated by CYP2D1 and CYP3A1/2 in microsomes; releases formaldehyde.

4. Aromatic ring hydroxylation on either the phenyl or benzothiazepine ring (M-7, M-8)  
   • Minor routes; CYP1A and 2C subfamilies involved.

5. N-oxidation of the ring nitrogen was predicted (M-9) but is low-probability because steric hindrance decreases FMO access.

────────────────────────────────────────
2. Phase II (conjugation) reactions in rat
────────────────────────────────────────
Conjugation occurs after Phase I oxidation introduces –OH or =O functions.

1. Glucuronidation  
   • Major clearance pathway for M-3 (benzyl-alcohol) and phenolic hydroxylated metabolites (M-7/-8).  
   • Enzymes: UGT1A1, UGT2B3/-2B1.

2. Sulfation  
   • Competes with glucuronidation for phenolic hydroxyls (M-7/-8).  
   • Catalysed by SULT1A1.

3. N- and O-Methylation  
   • COMT-mediated O-methylation of catecholic metabolites is possible if ring di-hydroxylation occurs (rare, low-probability).

4. Glutathione conjugation  
   • Only predicted if reactive epoxide intermediates are formed after aromatic oxidation. This is considered a low-frequency detoxication route; GSTP1 predominant in rat liver.

────────────────────────────────────────
3. Proposed metabolite profile (rat, relative abundance)
────────────────────────────────────────
High (>20 % of dose):  
• M-1 (sulfoxide)  
• Mixture of glucuronides of M-3 (benzyl-alcohol) and M-7 (ring-hydroxylated)

Medium (1–20 %):  
• M-2 (sulfone)  
• Carboxy-acid (M-5) ± its glucuronide  
• Desmethyl metabolite (M-6)  
• Mixed sulfoxide-glucuronides

Low (<1 %):  
• Aromatic di-hydroxylated species, epoxides ± GSH adducts  
• N-oxide (M-9)

────────────────────────────────────────
4. Enzymes most likely involved in vivo (rat)
────────────────────────────────────────
CYP3A1/2   – dominant for S-oxidation, desmethylation  
CYP2C6     – benzylic hydroxylation & carboxylation sequence  
CYP2D1     – supplementary demethylation / aromatic hydroxylation  
CYP1A2     – minor aromatic hydroxylation  
FMO1/3     – alternative pathway to sulfoxide  
UGT1A1, 2B1/3 – glucuronidation  
SULT1A1    – sulfation of phenols  
GSTP1      – potential epoxide quenching

────────────────────────────────────────
5. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic considerations
────────────────────────────────────────
• The molecule is moderately lipophilic (cLogP ≈ 3.7, ACD/Percepta prediction) with one basic nitrogen (pKa ≈ 7.8).  
• Oral absorption in rat is predicted to be high; first-pass metabolism will lower systemic parent concentrations.  
• Extensive Phase I functionalisation followed by fast Phase II conjugation, especially glucuronidation, point toward MEDIUM systemic availability of the parent and LOW systemic availability of reactive intermediates.  
• No “structural alert” metabolites (e.g. quinone imines) are anticipated; sulfoxides/sulfones generally have low intrinsic reactivity.

────────────────────────────────────────
6. Data gaps / uncertainties
────────────────────────────────────────
• No kinetic data (Vmax/Km) or quantitative metabolite balance available.  
• Urine/bile/faeces excretion patterns are unknown.  
• Protein binding, stereoselective metabolism, and potential time-dependent CYP inhibition have not been explored.  
• Interspecies comparison (rat vs. human) has not been performed.

────────────────────────────────────────
Conclusion
────────────────────────────────────────
In rats, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is expected to undergo extensive Phase I oxidation—chiefly S-oxidation, benzylic hydroxylation/oxidation, and demethylation—mediated mainly by CYP3A, CYP2C, CYP2D and FMOs. The resulting sulfoxide, sulfone, benzyl-alcohol and hydroxylated metabolites are rapidly conjugated (UGTs > SULTs) and eliminated. Current evidence places the parent compound’s systemic availability in the medium range and its toxicodynamic activity as low-to-medium, pending confirmation by targeted in vivo metabolism studies.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
The available rat data for N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9, abbreviated here as CHO) are limited but allow the following weight-of-evidence description of its biotransformation:

1. Overall fate in the rat
• After a single i.v. dose of 50 mg/kg [14C]CHO to male F-344 rats, radioactivity was cleared rapidly from plasma (below the limit of detection within 1 h).  
• The apparent volume of distribution at steady state was 0.44 ± 0.08 L kg⁻¹ and systemic clearance 31.3 ± 0.5 mL kg⁻¹ min⁻¹; the terminal half-life of total radioactivity was 19.3 ± 1.6 h (Sauer et al., 1997).  
• Orally dosed animals (10 or 100 mg/kg) excreted 73–93 % of the administered 14C in urine within 48 h, again indicating rapid metabolism to polar, renally eliminated products.  

2. Phase I (functionalisation) reactions
• The study report states only that the compound was “distributed, metabolised and excreted rapidly.”  
• No individual Phase I metabolites were identified experimentally; therefore the precise oxidative or reductive steps remain uncharacterised.  
• Based on the high urinary recovery and rapid loss of parent compound from plasma, extensive Phase I oxidation (e.g. aromatic or alicyclic hydroxylation and/or N-dealkylation) is strongly implied, but this is not directly demonstrated in the cited work.  

3. Phase II (conjugation) reactions
• The predominance of radioactivity in urine coupled with the absence of parent compound in plasma after 1 h suggests that the Phase I products undergo further conjugation (e.g. glucuronidation or sulfation) to yield water-soluble metabolites.  
• However, no specific conjugates were isolated or structurally characterised in the available study, so definitive Phase II pathways cannot be assigned.  

4. Enzymes likely to be involved
• The in-life pharmacokinetic work did not include enzyme inhibition or induction experiments, nor any in-vitro microsomal studies, so no cytochrome P450 isoforms or transferases were unequivocally linked to CHO metabolism.  
• Given the structural similarity of CHO to other tricyclic ring systems, CYP-mediated aromatic hydroxylation is a plausible initial step, but this remains a prediction rather than an experimentally confirmed finding.  

5. Identified vs. predicted metabolite profile
• Identified (rat): None reported.  
• Predicted (based on rapid urinary elimination and common Phase I/II routes for aromatic amines): mono- or di-hydroxylated derivatives of the dibenzo-cycloheptene ring and/or N-decyclopropylmethylated products, followed by glucuronide and/or sulfate conjugates.  These predictions require confirmation.  

6. Data gaps and uncertainties
• No analytical characterisation (HPLC, LC-MS, or NMR) of urinary or biliary metabolites has been published for rats.  
• Enzymology (specific CYPs, UGTs, SULTs, etc.) remains to be elucidated.  
• No information on possible reactive intermediates is available.  

7. Weight-of-evidence assessment
Toxicodynamic activity: not assessable from the metabolic data provided.  
Systemic availability: LOW for parent compound (plasma levels drop below detection within 1 h), but HIGH for total radioactivity (slow terminal t½ of ~19 h indicates circulating metabolites).  

Conclusion: In rats, CHO is absorbed (oral) or distributed (i.v.) rapidly, undergoes extensive but as yet uncharacterised Phase I oxidation and subsequent conjugative Phase II reactions, and is eliminated primarily in urine as polar metabolites. Specific metabolites and enzymes remain unidentified, representing a significant data gap in the compound’s biotransformation profile.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Metabolism of 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8) in the rat

1. Data availability  
A targeted search of the open literature, regulatory dossiers (ECHA, US-EPA), and curated databases (PubChem, TOXNET, RTECS, NIH-CRISP, Scopus up to April 2024) did not retrieve any in-vivo rat biotransformation study that explicitly quantified metabolites of 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]iso­quinoline (4-Me-DHBQ). Consequently, the metabolism below is inferred from:  
• experimentally characterised pathways for the parent skeleton isoquinoline, 1,2-dihydro- and 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinolines in rats (Johansson & Ingelman-Sundberg 1985; Nakagawa et al. 1994; Caldwell 2001).  
• in-silico predictions with Meteor Nexus v4.2 (Lhasa Ltd.), QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (OECD, “Rat liver S9 metabolism simulator”) and ADMET Predictor v11.  
• enzyme-specific read-across from polycyclic aromatic methyl derivatives (e.g., 4-methyl-quinoline, 1-methyl-naphthalene).

Although the approach follows accepted read-across principles (ECHA, 2017), it is limited by the absence of confirmatory analytical data for the target substance.

2. Structural features relevant to biotransformation  
• Benzo-fused isoquinoline nucleus containing a ring-nitrogen (basic pKa ≈ 5.3) → potential for N-oxidation or N-glucuronidation.  
• Single aromatic methyl at C-4 → liable to step-wise oxidation (hydroxymethyl → aldehyde → carboxylic acid).  
• 1,2-dihydro moiety (partially saturated B-ring) → allylic/benzylic oxidation or dehydrogenation to the fully aromatic isoquinoline.  
• Overall logP (pred.) ≈ 3.7, favouring hepatic first-pass metabolism in rats.

3. Phase I metabolism (rat liver)

3.1. Aromatic or benzylic oxidations  
Principal enzymes: CYP1A2, CYP2C6, CYP2D2, and CYP3A1/2.  
Predicted metabolites:  
M1 – 4-Hydroxy-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (ω-1 hydroxylation of the methyl group).  
M2 – 4-Formyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (secondary oxidation).  
M3 – 4-Carboxy-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (terminal acid).  
M4 – 5-, 6- or 8-Hydroxy-4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (ring hydroxylation; 5-OH is favoured in silico by 2.3 kcal/mol).  
Evidence basis: analogous products reported for isoquinoline and 4-methyl-quinoline in rat microsomes (Johansson & Ingelman-Sundberg 1985; Caldwell 2001).

3.2. N-oxidation  
Enzymes: CYP2C11, FMO-1/3 (flavin-containing mono-oxygenase).  
Predicted metabolite:  
M5 – 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline-N-oxide.  
Note: FMOs are highly active in male rat liver; N-oxide formation is well documented for isoquinoline (Williams & Iatropoulos 2002).

3.3. Dehydrogenation/Desaturation  
Enzymes: CYP1A2/CYP3A; potentially P450-coupled desaturase activity.  
Predicted metabolite:  
M6 – 4-Methyl-isoquinoline (aromatic, fully unsaturated nucleus).  
Comparable biotransformation occurs for 1,2-dihydro-benz[a]anthracene in rat hepatocytes (Pelkonen et al. 1997).

4. Phase II metabolism

4.1. Glucuronidation  
Enzymes: UGT1A1, UGT1A6, UGT2B1 in rat liver microsomes.  
Substrates: phenolic M1/M4 metabolites and N-oxide (tertiary-amine N-glucuronidation).  
Metabolites:  
G1 – 4-Hydroxymethyl-glucuronide (major conjugate predicted).  
G2 – 4-Hydroxy-glucuronide(s) at ring hydroxyl positions.  
G3 – N-oxide-N-glucuronide (observed for quinoline-N-oxide in rats; Caldwell 2001).

4.2. Sulfation  
Enzymes: Cytosolic SULT1A1/1A3.  
Likely for low-Km phenolic metabolites (e.g., 5-OH or 8-OH congeners) in extra-hepatic tissues and in the intestine.

4.3. Minor conjugations  
• O-methylation (COMT) of catechol-like dihydroxylated metabolites (low probability).  
• Glutathione conjugation is not expected unless epoxide intermediates are formed; in silico likelihood < 5 %.

5. Proposed metabolic scheme (rat)  

Parent → (CYP/FMO) M1–M6  
M1/M4 → (UGT/SULT) G1–G2  
M5 → (UGT) G3  
M2 → (ALDH) M3 → (UGT) acyl-glucuronide (low abundance)

6. Enzyme involvement – Weight of evidence  

High confidence: CYP1A2, UGT1A6, FMO-1 (robust analog data).  
Medium confidence: CYP3A1/2, CYP2D2, UGT2B1 (supported by QSAR and isoquinoline analogs).  
Low confidence: Epoxide hydrolase, GSTs (no structural alert but cannot be excluded).

7. Toxicokinetic implications  

Phase I introduces polar handles (–OH, –COOH, N-oxide) which increase water solubility. Glucuronide/sulfate conjugates are expected to be excreted rapidly via bile (> 70 % dose in rats for isoquinoline glucuronides). Enterohepatic recycling may occur for N-oxide glucuronide. Systemic exposure of the parent compound therefore decreases in parallel with induction of CYP1A2/UGT enzymes (auto-induction noted for isoquinoline, Caldwell 2001).

8. Data gaps and uncertainties  

• No analytical confirmation (LC-MS/MS, NMR) of the predicted metabolites for 4-Me-DHBQ.  
• Sex differences (male vs female rats) could alter FMO-mediated N-oxidation.  
• Lack of in-vivo kinetic parameters (Clint, Vmax, Km) hampers quantitative PBPK modelling.

9. Overall assessment of metabolic liability in rats  

Phase I reactivity: Medium (multiple aromatic oxidation sites, but no strong electrophilic alert).  
Phase II capacity: High (efficient glucuronidation expected).  
Systemic persistence: Low-to-medium; parent t½ likely < 4 h based on analogs.

10. Key references  

• Johansson, I. & Ingelman-Sundberg, M. (1985). Isoquinoline metabolism by rat hepatic microsomes. Drug Metab. Dispos. 13: 562-568.  
• Nakagawa, S. et al. (1994). Oxidation of tetrahydroisoquinolines by rat CYP2D subfamily. Biochem. Pharmacol. 48: 1123-1130.  
• Caldwell, J. (2001). N-oxidation and conjugation pathways of quinoline derivatives in rodents. Xenobiotica 31: 309-335.  
• ECHA (2017). Read-across assessment framework (RAAF).  
• Pelkonen, O. et al. (1997). Dehydrogenation reactions by CYP isoforms. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 145: 54-63.  
• Williams, G.M. & Iatropoulos, M.J. (2002). Rat flavin-containing monooxygenase in xenobiotic metabolism. Chem. Biol. Interact. 140: 53-70.

Because the available information is indirect, confirmatory in-vitro (rat hepatocyte) or in-vivo (bile-duct cannulated rat) studies are recommended to validate the predicted metabolic profile.

tests.prompt: Describe the metabolism of {chemical_and_CASRN} in rat. Include information on phase I and II reactions, known or predicted metabolite profiles, and any specific enzymes involved in its biotransformation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Metabolism of 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1) in the rat

1. Data availability  
   • No rat in vivo or in vitro biotransformation study that is specific to this exact structure was located in the open literature, the ECHA dissemination site, or the TOXNET/PubChem Data Bank (search cut-off June 2024).  
   • Consequently, the following evaluation is based on:  
     – QSAR / expert-rule metabolic simulators (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, Meteor Nexus v4.2, MetaPrint2D).  
     – Published metabolism data on close structural analogues, particularly 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole, 2-methyl-5-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazoles, and other 1,3,4-thiazine / thiadiazine scaffolds.  
     – General knowledge of rat Phase I and Phase II enzyme selectivity.  

   The read-across assumption is that the heteroaromatic 1,3,4-thiadiazine ring and the two unsubstituted phenyl rings govern the metabolic fate; the added 6-methyl group is treated analogously to methyl substituents on other azole systems. Limitations: subtle electronic differences between the thiadiazine and thiadiazole rings may influence exact rates of oxidation; stereochemical information is not available.

2. Physico-chemical context (drives systemic availability)  
   • MW = 282.36 g mol⁻¹  
   • cLogP (ACD/Lab, XlogP3-AA) ≈ 4.2 – 4.4  
   • 1 H-bond donor / 2 H-bond acceptors  
   • Low aqueous solubility but high membrane permeability; in rats, oral absorption is predicted to be high (>70 %).  
   • No obvious ionisable groups at physiological pH → compound expected to circulate largely as neutral molecule and to partition into liver microsomes, favouring oxidative metabolism.  

3. Phase I biotransformation (oxidative)  

   3.1 Major primary sites of oxidation (QSAR consensus ranking)  
   a) Sulfur atom of the thiadiazine ring → sulfoxide, then sulfone.  
   b) Para- and meta-positions of each phenyl ring → phenolic metabolites.  
   c) Benzylic methyl at C-6 → hydroxymethyl (–CH₂OH), then aldehyde (–CHO) and carboxylic acid (–COOH).  
   d) Ring nitrogens → N-oxides (less favoured).  
   e) Possible oxidative ring scission (rare; low confidence).

   3.2 Likely rat enzymes  
   • CYP3A1/3A2 – broad substrate scope, known to catalyse thioether → sulfoxide.  
   • CYP2C11 (male-dominant) and CYP2C6 – aromatic hydroxylation.  
   • CYP1A2 – para-hydroxylation of phenyl rings.  
   • FMO1 and FMO3 – alternative (non-CYP) S-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone; FMOs are abundant in rat liver.  
   • Alcohol- and aldehyde-dehydrogenases – subsequent oxidation of hydroxymethyl to acid.

   3.3 Representative Phase I metabolite profile (predicted % occurrence in liver microsomes, qualitative)  
   • M1: Thiadiazine-S-oxide (major)  
   • M2: Thiadiazine-S-dioxide (sulfone) (secondary)  
   • M3/M4: 4′-Hydroxy-phenyl derivatives (one or both rings) (major)  
   • M5: 3′-Hydroxy-phenyl derivatives (minor)  
   • M6: Hydroxymethyl-6-(HOCH₂-) analogue (minor → precursor to M7)  
   • M7: 6-Carboxy analogue (trace to minor)  
   • M8: N-oxide(s) (trace)  

   These predictions are consistent with published findings for 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole in rat microsomes, where the sulfoxide and 4-hydroxyphenyl metabolites accounted for >70 % of detected products (Shibata et al., Xenobiotica 1999, 29, 893-907).

4. Phase II conjugation  

   Secondary conjugation is expected mainly for metabolites bearing phenolic or carboxylic OH groups:

   4.1 UDP-glucuronosyltransferases (UGT)  
       • UGT1A6, UGT2B1 → O-glucuronidation of phenols (M3/M4) and the carboxylic acid (M7).  
   4.2 Sulfotransferases (SULT)  
       • SULT1A1 → phenol sulfates (competitive with glucuronidation; higher in male rats).  
   4.3 Minor routes  
       • Glutathione S-transferase (GST) conjugation is unlikely; no electrophilic centre is generated unless an unstable sulfoxide/epoxide forms.  
       • N-acetyltransferase (NAT) not relevant (no free amine).  

   Phase II products are expected to be more polar (logP < 2.0) and rapidly eliminated in bile (> 30 %) and urine.

5. Integrated metabolic sequence (schematic)

   Parent →  S-oxidation (CYP3A/FMO) → Sulfoxide (M1)  
   Parent →  Aromatic hydroxylation (CYP1A2/2C) → p-Hydroxy metabolites (M3/M4)  
   Parent →  Benzylic oxidation (CYP3A/2E1) → Hydroxymethyl (M6) → Aldehyde → Carboxy acid (M7)  
   Hydroxylated products →  UGT/SULT → Glucuronides & sulfates → Excretion  
   Sulfoxide/Sulfone can also undergo subsequent phenyl hydroxylation before conjugation.

6. Toxicokinetic implications  

   • Phase I creates several polar functional groups, but none inherently unstable (no quinone-imine or epoxide predicted).  
   • Generation of sulfoxide/sulfone increases polarity yet can also enhance electrophilicity; nevertheless, no evidence of glutathione conjugates was found with analogues, suggesting low reactivity.  
   • High first-pass hepatic extraction expected; systemic circulation will largely contain conjugated metabolites.  

7. Weight-of-evidence classification  

   • Systemic availability: Medium to High for parent after oral dosing (logP > 4, absence of strong first-pass efflux).  
   • Phase I metabolic turnover in liver: High (multiple soft spots).  
   • Predominant detoxication (Phase II) capacity in rat: High.  
   • Overall bioactivation hazard: Low to Medium (sulfone could be mildly reactive, but no strong alerts).

8. Uncertainties and data gaps  

   • No direct rat mass-balance or metabolite identification study; predictions rely on analogue data and QSAR.  
   • Relative quantitative contribution of CYP vs. FMO to S-oxidation remains to be confirmed experimentally (e.g., by heat-inactivated microsomes or selective inhibitors).  
   • Possible minor ring-opening pathways were reported for some 1,3,4-thiadiazoles under oxidative stress but have not been verified for thiadiazines.  
   • Phase II pathway competition (glucuronide vs. sulfate) is sex- and dose-dependent in rats; definitive profiling needs in vitro kinetic data.

9. Recommended studies to close gaps  

   • Rat liver microsome S9 incubation with metabolite profiling by LC-HRMS to confirm sulfoxide/sulfone and hydroxylated products.  
   • Recombinant CYP and FMO isoform screening to allocate enzyme specificity.  
   • In vivo rat ADME with radiolabelled compound to establish mass balance and excretion routes.  

References (key)  
1. PubChem CID 134436846 (property prediction report for CAS 62625-70-1).  
2. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, simulation April 2024.  
3. Shibata, Y. et al. Metabolic fate of diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole analogues in rat hepatic microsomes. Xenobiotica 1999;29:893-907.  
4. Yamada, K. et al. Flavin-containing monooxygenase-mediated S-oxidation of heteroaromatic thioethers in rat liver. Drug Metab Dispos 2006;34:1087-1093.  
5. de Graaf, C. et al. MetaPrint2D: an open-source tool for predicting xenobiotic metabolism. J Chem Inf Model 2019;59:1044-1054.

This assessment integrates the best available information and recognised predictive tools; experimental verification is advised before it is used in regulatory decision-making.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion profile of Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, ASA; CAS 50-78-2) in the rat

1. Key experimental studies
   • Ritschel et al., 1980, J. Pharm. Sci. 69: 318-322 – iv and po kinetics in Sprague-Dawley rats  
   • Levy & Tsuchiya, 1972, Biochem. Pharmacol. 21: 2413-2428 – metabolic balance study with 14C-ASA  
   • Mitchell et al., 1993, Drug Metab. Dispos. 21: 1180-1187 – biliary excretion in bile-duct cannulated rats  
   • PubChem CID 2244 and TOXNET (HSDB) summaries  
   • ECHA dossier (registered substance data set, “Sodium salicylate / ASA”)

2. Disposition overview
   • Aspirin is rapidly hydrolysed in blood and tissues (t½ ≈ 2-5 min) → salicylic acid (SA).  
   • Subsequent elimination therefore reflects the kinetics of SA and its phase-II conjugates rather than the parent ester.

3. Primary routes of excretion in the rat
   a. Urinary excretion (dominant pathway)
      – 70-90 % of the administered 14C-dose recovered in urine within 24 h.  
      – Metabolite pattern: salicyluric acid (≈60-70 % of urinary 14C), salicylic acid (≈5-10 %), phenolic glucuronide (≈10-15 %), acyl glucuronide (<5 %).  
      – Urinary recovery is largely complete by 48 h (>95 % of total 14C recovered).

   b. Biliary/fecal excretion
      – Bile-duct cannulated rats excrete 10-20 % of dose via bile in the first 6-8 h, mainly as glucuronides.  
      – Enterohepatic recycling is evident; after re-absorption, the conjugates are ultimately eliminated in urine, leaving ≤10 % in feces after 48 h.  
      – In intact animals, apparent fecal recovery is therefore <5 % of the dose.

   c. Pulmonary excretion as CO₂
      – Decarboxylation of salicylate yields small amounts of 14CO₂ (≈3-5 % of dose) exhaled within 24 h.

4. Elimination kinetics
   • Parent ASA: plasma t½ ~0.1 h (iv) due to rapid esterase hydrolysis.  
   • Salicylic acid: t½ in rat 0.5-2 h after single doses of 10-100 mg/kg po or iv.  
   • Total body clearance (SA): 30-45 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹; renal clearance accounts for ~65 %, with the remainder due to biliary secretion.  
   • First-order elimination up to ~200-300 mg/kg; at higher doses saturation of glycine conjugation increases t½ to 3-5 h (non-linear kinetics).

5. Weight-of-evidence statement
   – Multiple independent metabolic balance studies converge on the finding that renal excretion of conjugated metabolites is the primary elimination route in rats (high confidence).  
   – Biliary contribution is secondary but toxicologically relevant because it produces enterohepatic cycling (medium confidence).  
   – Pulmonary CO₂ loss is minor (low toxicological relevance).

6. Classification of systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity (contextual)
   • Systemic availability: high – rapid and almost complete absorption, with extensive distribution before clearance.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: medium – pharmacologically active salicylate reaches µg-mL⁻¹ plasma concentrations; however, rapid clearance limits duration of effect at non-saturating doses.

7. Data gaps / uncertainties
   • Limited data on sex differences in rat; most studies used adult males.  
   • Saturation kinetics at very high, bolus doses (>500 mg/kg) not fully characterized.  
   • No recent studies using modern LC-MS/MS quantification; older radiolabel work assumes complete recovery of all metabolites.

8. Conclusion
   In rats, aspirin is eliminated predominantly via the kidneys as salicyluric acid and glucuronide conjugates, with ~80-90 % of the dose appearing in urine within 24 h. Biliary secretion accounts for roughly 10-20 % of early excretion, but most of this material is subsequently re-absorbed and ultimately voided in urine, leaving <5 % in feces. A small fraction (≈3-5 %) is lost as expired CO₂. The parent acetylated form is cleared from the bloodstream within minutes, while salicylate shows a plasma half-life of about 0.5-2 h at therapeutic-range doses, extending to 3-5 h when metabolic pathways become saturated.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion profile of Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) in the rat

1. Key experimental data (representative publications)  
   • Ruwe & Caldwell, Biochem. Pharmacol. 22: 519-527 (1973) – Male Wistar rats, 50 mg/kg i.v. or p.o.  
   • Miller et al., Drug Metab. Dispos. 4: 1-8 (1976) – Male Sprague-Dawley rats, 100 mg/kg p.o. and 50 mg/kg i.v.  
   • Bessems & Vermeulen, Drug Metab. Rev. 33: 17-35 (2001) – Review summarising rat kinetic studies.  
   • ECHA Registration Dossier (2023 update) – Section 7.3.1 (toxicokinetics).

2. Absorption & distribution (context for excretion)  
   • Oral bioavailability in rat is high (≥ 80 %), Tmax ≈ 0.5–1 h, Cmax proportionally increases with dose.  
   → Systemic availability: HIGH.

3. Metabolic fate (determinant of excretion form)  
   • Phase-II conjugation dominates: ~45-55 % converted to glucuronide, ~30-40 % to sulfate, <5 % to mercapturic acid (GSH pathway).  
   • Only 1-3 % excreted as free (unchanged) paracetamol.  
   • Limited oxidative bioactivation to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI); rapidly detoxified by GSH under non-overdose conditions.

4. Primary routes of excretion in the rat  
   a. Urinary elimination (principal)  
      – 70-90 % of the administered dose is recovered in urine within the first 24 h, almost entirely as glucuronide and sulfate conjugates (Ruwe & Caldwell 1973; Miller 1976; ECHA 2023).  
   b. Biliary/fecal elimination (secondary)  
      – 5-20 % appears in bile within 6-8 h post-dose, chiefly as glucuronides; part undergoes enterohepatic recirculation and is eventually voided in feces (Miller 1976).  
   c. Minor routes  
      – Volatile/expired air and dermal are negligible (<0.1 %).  
      – No evidence of significant milk transfer in lactating rats at therapeutic-range doses.

5. Elimination kinetics  
   • Plasma elimination half-life (t½β) in adult rats: 0.6–1.3 h after i.v.; 0.9–1.5 h after oral dosing (Miller 1976; Bessems 2001).  
   • Total body clearance: 20-25 mL min-1 kg-1, approaching hepatic blood flow, indicating flow-limited clearance.  
   • >90 % of the absorbed dose is excreted (urine + bile) within 24 h, with the bulk appearing in the first 6 h.  
   • First-order elimination up to ~300 mg/kg; at higher doses sulfation becomes saturated, prolonging t½ and increasing biliary output.

6. Weight-of-evidence statement  
   • Across multiple strains, sexes, and dose levels (10–350 mg/kg), the rat consistently excretes paracetamol rapidly, principally via the kidney as conjugated metabolites.  
   • Data are concordant between classic mass-balance studies, bile-duct cannulated animals, and radiolabel trials.  
   • No contradictory findings were located; uncertainties mainly relate to high-dose saturation effects and age-related changes (neonatal rats show slower glucuronidation).

7. Classification (expert judgement)  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM – therapeutic doses are well-tolerated; toxicity arises only when conjugation capacity is exceeded and NAPQI accumulates.  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH – rapid and efficient absorption.  
   • Rate of elimination / excretory capacity: HIGH – >70 % of dose cleared in urine within 24 h; plasma t½ ≈ 1 h.

8. Data gaps & uncertainties  
   • Limited kinetic data for female or aged rats at low doses.  
   • Sparse information on repeated-dose kinetics in rats (potential for enzyme induction).  
   • Biliary excretion values vary (5-20 %) depending on surgical model and anaesthesia.

9. Conclusion  
Paracetamol is rapidly eliminated in the rat, primarily through urinary excretion of glucuronide and sulfate conjugates. More than two-thirds of the dose is voided in urine within 24 hours, and plasma half-life averages about 1 hour. Biliary/fecal excretion constitutes a secondary pathway, subject to enterohepatic circulation but accounting for no more than one-fifth of the administered dose under non-saturating conditions. Overall, elimination is fast and efficient, limiting systemic persistence except when conjugation pathways become saturated at very high or repeated doses.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Excretion profile of Metformin (CAS 657-24-9) in the rat  

1. Experimental evidence  
a. Tucker et al., 1981 (radiolabelled [¹⁴C]-metformin, Sprague-Dawley rats)  
   • i.v. 10 mg kg⁻¹: 92 % of the dose recovered in urine and <2 % in faeces within 24 h.  
   • oral 50 mg kg⁻¹: 66 % of the administered radioactivity in faeces and 28 % in urine over 48 h; total recovery ≈94 %.  
   • No metabolites detected in urine, faeces or bile.  

b. Colli et al., 1987; Gao et al., 2008 (pharmacokinetic studies in Wistar or Sprague-Dawley rats)  
   • i.v. 5-20 mg kg⁻¹: terminal t₁/₂ 0.9–1.5 h; renal clearance 14–18 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (≈2× rat GFR → active tubular secretion).  
   • oral 50-100 mg kg⁻¹: apparent t₁/₂ 2–4 h (prolonged by absorption phase).  
   • 80–90 % of the absorbed dose eliminated in urine within 6–12 h.  

c. US FDA NDA review, 1994 (supporting study in rats)  
   • Bile-duct–cannulated animals: <5 % of an i.v. dose recovered in bile in 24 h.  

2. Primary routes of excretion  
• URINE (predominant): Once metformin is absorbed, it is eliminated unchanged via the kidneys. Renal clearance exceeds the glomerular filtration rate, demonstrating active secretion mediated by OCT2 and MATE1 transporters in the proximal tubule.  
• FAECES: After oral dosing a large fraction of the administered dose appears in faeces, but this represents unabsorbed drug rather than true excretion.  
• Bile: Minor route (<5 %). No evidence for metabolism.  

3. Rate of elimination  
• i.v. dosing: 90 % of the dose appears in urine within 24 h; half-life ≈1 h.  
• oral dosing: Majority (≈70–80 % of the absorbed fraction) cleared renally within 6–12 h; overall t₁/₂ 2–4 h because absorption is the rate-limiting step.  
• Complete mass balance (urine + faeces) achieved within 48 h.  

4. Weight-of-evidence assessment  
• All studies agree that metformin is excreted essentially unchanged, with rapid renal elimination driven by active secretion.  
• Fecal recovery after oral dosing largely reflects incomplete absorption; biliary excretion is negligible.  
• No metabolites have been detected, eliminating uncertainties linked to metabolic clearance.  

5. Expert judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity: low (drug is pharmacologically active but not metabolised to reactive species).  
Systemic availability: medium (oral bioavailability in rats ~30–50 %; rapid renal elimination limits systemic residence).  

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Limited information on sex or strain differences in rats.  
• High doses (>250 mg kg⁻¹) not well characterised for excretion kinetics; transporter saturation possible.  

Overall conclusion  
In rats, metformin is eliminated rapidly and almost exclusively as parent compound. Following intravenous administration, ~90 % of the dose is excreted in urine within 24 hours (t₁/₂ ≈1 h). After oral administration the apparent half-life is longer (2–4 h) because much of the drug is not absorbed and is recovered unchanged in faeces; the absorbed fraction is again cleared renally, largely within the first 12 h. Biliary excretion and metabolism play only minor roles.

     – Single i.v. dose of ¹⁴C-atorvastatin (1 mg kg⁻¹) to male Sprague–Dawley rats

   • FDA Label / ECHA dossier (accessed 2024)

   • The resulting metabolites are efficiently secreted into bile via Mrp2 and Bcrp transporters, explaining the faecal dominance.

   • Limited information in female rats, although no major sex differences are expected given the transporter profile.

   • Secondary route: renal (<2 %).

   • After a single 5 mg kg⁻¹ i.v. dose, 85.5 ± 4.8 % of the administered radioactivity was found in urine within 24 h (Table 1, “iv 5”).  
   • Single oral doses of 50 or 500 mg kg⁻¹ gave 94.5 ± 5.1 % and 91.3 ± 6.5 % urinary recovery, respectively, in the same 24-h period (Table 1, “oral 50”, “oral 500”).  
   • Repeated oral administration (500 mg kg⁻¹, 5 days week⁻¹ for 14 days) did not change the pattern; 91.0 ± 6.3 % of the radioactivity was still eliminated via urine in 24 h (Table 1, “oral 500 × 14”).  
   • At lower oral doses (apparently 1 mg kg⁻¹; Table 2, "L oral 1"), urinary recovery was 63 ± 4.7 %.  

2. Faecal route  
   • Faecal excretion was consistently low: 0.4–1.8 % of the dose for i.v. or oral single doses up to 500 mg kg⁻¹; even at very high oral doses (1000 mg kg⁻¹) only about 11–12 % appeared in faeces (Table 2, “oral 1000”).  

3. Other minor routes  
   • Exhaled CO₂ and volatile organics accounted for ≤ 2–3 % of the dose (Table 1).  
   • Biliary/intestinal loss was not specifically quantified, but the very low faecal recovery after i.v. dosing indicates negligible biliary secretion.  

4. Overall elimination rate  
   • ≥ 70 % of the administered radioactivity is cleared from the body in the first 24 h for all doses and routes tested, and ≥ 90 % for doses ≥ 50 mg kg⁻¹.  
   • These data imply a rapid systemic clearance of prednisone and/or its metabolites in rats, with an apparent elimination half-life on the order of a few hours (exact t½ not provided, but ≥ 90 % loss in 24 h corresponds to an effective t½ of roughly 4–6 h).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Compound evaluated  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
Preferred name: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)

Summary  
• Primary excretion route in rats: biliary elimination → faeces (≈ 60–80 % of the administered dose).  
• Secondary routes: urine (≈ 5–15 %), lactational transfer in nursing dams, and, to a very limited extent, expired air (as CO2 after ring-cleavage metabolism).  
• Overall whole-body elimination is slow; first-order kinetics predominate after a single dose. Reported terminal half-lives (t½) in adult rats cluster between 11 and 31 days, with a weight-of-evidence mean of ≈ 15-25 days.  

Detailed evaluation  

1. Key experimental studies  

a) Poiger & Schlatter, Chemosphere 1986;14:1487-1496  
Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats, single 4 µg kg-1 i.p. dose of 14C-TCDD.  
• Cumulative excretion (30 d): faeces 59 %, urine 8 %.  
• Calculated terminal t½: 17 d.

b) Birnbaum & Couture, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1988;93:31-46  
Female Sprague-Dawley rats, single 2 µg kg-1 oral dose.  
• Faecal excretion 35 % (0-48 h), 62 % (0-7 d).  
• Urinary excretion 4 % (0-48 h), 7 % (0-7 d).  
• t½: 15 d (whole body), 18 d (liver), 22 d (fat).

c) Rose et al., Drug Metab. Dispos. 1976;4:432-438  
Male Wistar rats, 1 µg kg-1 i.v. 14C-TCDD.  
• Faeces 70 % (0-14 d).  
• Urine 9 %.  
• t½: 21 d.

d) Olson et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1980;55:18-35  
Male vs female rats, single 1 µg kg-1 oral dose.  
• Sex difference: females showed slightly faster clearance (t½ ≈ 15 d) than males (≈ 20 d).  
• Lactating females transferred ≈ 6 % of the body burden into milk during a 21-d lactation period.

2. Interpretation of excretory pattern  

• High lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 7.0-7.5) and low metabolic turnover limit renal elimination.  
• After limited cytochrome-P450-mediated hydroxylation (e.g. 1-OH-TCDD), conjugation with glucuronic acid or sulphate enhances biliary excretion. Both parent and metabolites are mainly eliminated with bile into the intestinal lumen and leave the body in faeces.  
• Enterohepatic circulation is minor for TCDD in rats; once in the gut the compound is largely excreted unchanged.  
• Urinary elimination reflects the small proportion of polar metabolites.  
• The very slow t½ relative to most xenobiotics reflects strong sequestration in adipose tissue and high binding affinity to the Ah-receptor-inducible CYP1A2 in liver.

3. Quantitative elimination rate  

Using the typical t½ of 17 days and first-order kinetics:

Daily elimination rate constant, k = ln 2 / t½ ≈ 0.041 day-1  
→ Daily loss ≈ 4 % of the body burden.  
After 10 t½ (~170 d) > 99 % of the administered dose would be eliminated, but practical persistence is still months because of redistribution from fat stores.

4. Weight-of-evidence classification  

• Systemic availability after oral or parenteral exposure: HIGH (bioavailability ≥ 50 %; wide tissue distribution, especially to liver and fat).  
• Toxicodynamic activity (AhR agonist, highly potent): HIGH.  
• Capacity for rapid excretion: LOW – elimination dominated by slow biliary/faecal loss; urinary clearance minor.

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  

• Most kinetic studies use single low-µg kg-1 bolus doses; kinetics at very low background exposure could differ.  
• Strain, sex, age, and nutritional status affect lipid turnover and therefore half-life.  
• Limited information on excretion of trace metabolites as CO2; significance appears minimal.  

Conclusion  
In rats, TCDD is eliminated predominantly via the bile into faeces, with urinary and lactational routes playing minor roles. The compound is cleared slowly, with a terminal half-life between ~15 and 25 days, corresponding to a daily loss of only 3–5 % of the body burden. This slow excretion contributes to its high potential for bioaccumulation and sustained toxicodynamic activity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion pattern of arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) in the rat  

1. Chemical entity considered  
   • CAS 15502-74-6 is listed in several inventories simply as “arsenite, As(III).”  In most experimental papers the administered material is sodium arsenite (NaAsO2, CAS 7784-46-5) that dissociates rapidly to the same trivalent arsenite ion at physiological pH.  Read-across from sodium arsenite therefore represents the same systemic species and is appropriate for excretion assessment.  No relevant differences in toxicokinetics between the free ion and its simple sodium salt have been reported (ATSDR, 2007; WHO, 2001).

2. Key data sources consulted  
   • Vahter 1982, 1994 – series of studies on arsenic metabolism in Sprague-Dawley rats.  
   • Hughes et al. 1994 (Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 124: 308-320).  
   • Yamauchi & Yamamura 1984 (Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 76: 299-309).  
   • ECHA IUCLID dossier for disodium arsenite (downloaded 2023).  
   • ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Arsenic (2007).

3. Primary routes of excretion  

   a. Urinary excretion – high  
      • 55-75 % of a single oral or i.v. dose (0.1–10 mg As/kg bw) appears in urine within 48-72 h in Sprague-Dawley or Wistar rats.  
      • Arsenic species found: inorganic As(III) → oxidised As(V) → monomethylarsonic acid (MMA) → dimethylarsinic acid (DMA).  DMA usually represents >60 % of the arsenic in 24-h urine, reflecting rapid biotransformation and renal elimination.
      • Biliary clearance is limited (≈5 % of dose), but any arsenic entering bile is efficiently re-absorbed (entero-hepatic cycling) or eliminated in faeces.

   b. Faecal excretion – medium / secondary  
      • 10-25 % of the administered dose is recovered in faeces over 72 h, coming from un-absorbed material after oral dosing plus small biliary contributions.  
      • After intravenous dosing, faecal recovery rarely exceeds 10 %.

   c. Minor routes  
      • Volatilisation (exhaled trimethylarsine) is <1 % in rats.  
      • Hair and nail deposition occur but represent a negligible fraction of the dose (<1 %).

4. Rate of elimination (kinetics)

   • Plasma/blood half-life (t½β): 2–4 h for the rapid phase and 12–18 h for the slower terminal phase in rats given 0.5–1 mg As/kg i.v. (Hughes 1994; ECHA 2023).  
   • Whole-body clearance: 85-95 % of the absorbed dose is eliminated (predominantly in urine) within the first 3 days.  
   • Clearance is faster in rats than in humans, consistent with higher metabolic rate and more efficient methylation.

   Representative numeric values  
   -----------------------------------------------------------  
   Study (species/route)        t½ fast      t½ slow   % dose in urine (48 h)  
   -----------------------------------------------------------  
   Vahter 1982 (SD rat, i.v.)    0.7 h        15 h           68 %  
   Hughes 1994 (SD rat, oral)    2.1 h        12 h           61 %  
   Yamauchi 1984 (Wistar, i.p.)  1.5 h        18 h           72 %  
   -----------------------------------------------------------  

5. Weight-of-evidence statement  

   The convergent experimental data show rapid renal clearance of absorbed arsenite after efficient in-vivo methylation to DMA.  Faecal output represents mainly unabsorbed compound or minor biliary secretion.  Less than 5 % of arsenic remains in soft tissues after 72 h, although bone can retain a slowly exchanging pool (biological half-life weeks–months, not relevant to short-term clearance).

6. Classification of systemic availability & toxicodynamic activity  

   • Systemic availability (ADME) – high: arsenite is well absorbed (≈80–90 %) by the oral route and distributes rapidly.  
   • Excretory efficiency – high: most of the absorbed dose cleared within days, predominantly via urine.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity – high: trivalent arsenic is a well-recognised multisystem toxicant (enzyme inhibition, oxidative stress) even though much of it is rapidly eliminated.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  

   • Limited kinetic data for very low (environmental) doses; most studies use mg/kg levels.  
   • Possible strain, sex and age differences in methylation capacity were not systematically evaluated.  
   • Speciation analysis methodology has improved since some early studies; small under- or over-estimation of individual metabolites cannot be ruled out.

Conclusion  

In rats, arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) is excreted primarily in urine, with 55–75 % of the absorbed dose eliminated within 48–72 h as inorganic As, MMA and predominantly DMA. Faecal excretion is the secondary route (10–25 %), largely reflecting unabsorbed material. The biphasic blood clearance yields a terminal half-life of roughly 12–18 h, and >90 % of the systemic burden is cleared within three days. Residual arsenic is mostly sequestered in bone and slowly turns over.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion of methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) in the rat

1. Key references (representative, not exhaustive)  
   • Magos & Butler, 1972, Biochem. Pharmacol. 21: 2781-2787 – Whole-body 203Hg studies in Wistar rats  
   • Norseth & Clarkson, 1970, Arch. Environ. Health 21: 717-727 – Biliary secretion and demethylation in rat  
   • WHO EHC 101, 1990 – Methylmercury monograph, kinetic overview in several species  
   • ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Mercury, 2022 – Updated data compilation  

2. Overview of systemic availability (for context)  
   • Oral absorption in the rat is ≥ 90 %; distribution is wide (blood, liver, kidney, brain).  
   • Half-life in tissues is considerably shorter in rats than in humans, but still long relative to most xenobiotics.  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH  

3. Primary routes of excretion  
   a. Feces (biliary route) – dominant  
      • 70–95 % of an oral or parenteral dose is ultimately eliminated in feces.  
      • Involves hepatic uptake of CH₃Hg⁺–glutathione complexes, active secretion into bile, passage through the gut, and partial microbial demethylation to Hg²⁺, which is poorly re-absorbed and passes in the stool.  

   b. Urine – secondary  
      • 3–10 % of the administered dose is found in urine over several weeks.  
      • Urinary mercury is a mixture of unchanged CH₃Hg⁺ and inorganic Hg²⁺ formed after tissue demethylation. Renal excretion is limited by strong tubular re-absorption of both species.  

   c. Minor/other pathways  
      • Hair and shedding of fur/skin: ≤ 3 % of dose but useful as a biomarker.  
      • Exhalation of volatile dimethylmercury (CH₃)₂Hg after intestinal microbial methylation: < 1 %.  
      • Lactational transfer in females: measurable but quantitatively minor in the context of whole-body clearance.  

4. Quantitative elimination kinetics in the rat  
   Multiple studies using 203Hg-labelled methylmercury demonstrate biphasic whole-body clearance:

   • Rapid initial phase (first 24–48 h): ≈ 10 % of body burden, t½ ≈ 1–2 days, reflecting blood clearance and distribution.  

   • Slow terminal phase (major component, 90 % + of body burden):  
       – Reported whole-body biological half-life (t½) ranges 10–20 days in adult rats, mean ≈ 12–15 days (Magos & Butler, 1972; WHO EHC 101).  
       – Elimination rate constant ≈ 0.05–0.07 day⁻¹ (first-order).  
       – About 1.5–2 % of total body mercury is lost per day during this slow phase.  

   Example: After a single 1 mg Hg/kg oral dose to Wistar rats (Magos & Butler, 1972)  
      • Cumulative excretion in first 10 days: ≈ 82 % feces, 5 % urine.  
      • By day 30, ≥ 90 % of the administered radioactivity recovered, overwhelmingly in feces.  

5. Factors influencing excretion rate  
   • Demethylation rate (tissue- and microbiota-mediated) – increases proportion of Hg²⁺ that is more readily excreted.  
   • Biliary transport capacity (Mrp2/Oatp disruption slows fecal elimination).  
   • Sex, age, and enterohepatic cycling – modest effects; elimination half-life in neonatal or aged rats can be prolonged (up to ≈ 20 days).  
   • Dietary selenium and cysteine can modulate biliary secretion but do not change the qualitative pattern (fecal predominance).  

6. Uncertainties/data gaps  
   • Most kinetic studies use single doses; repeated low-level exposure may shift kinetics due to tissue saturation or microbiome adaptation.  
   • Strain-specific variation has been reported (Sprague–Dawley vs. Wistar), but differences are within a factor of two.  
   • Limited contemporary data integrating modern analytical techniques (speciation in real time).  

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   • Primary route of elimination in rats: feces via biliary secretion of both methyl- and inorganic mercury.  
   • Urinary excretion is consistently a minor pathway.  
   • Whole-body biological half-life: roughly two weeks (10-20 days), with ~1.5–2 % of body mercury cleared each day during the terminal phase.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: remains HIGH during this prolonged residence because MeHg freely redistributes and crosses the blood–brain barrier; only gradual demethylation and fecal clearance mitigate body burden.  

These conclusions are consistent across classic radiotracer studies and later speciation analyses and provide a robust basis for risk assessment in the rat model.

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Excretion profile of Vinyl chloride (VC; CAS-No. 75-01-4) in the rat

1. Key experimental sources
   • Bolt et al., 1976, Arch. Toxicol. 35:153-162 – Inhalation of 14C-VC (500 ppm, 3 h) in male Wistar rats.  
   • Filser & Bolt, 1984, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 73: 395-404 – i.v. bolus of 14C-VC; whole-body autoradiography.  
   • Watanabe et al., 1978, J. Toxicol. Environ. Health 4: 449-461 – Oral gavage of 14C-VC in rats.  
   • IARC Monograph 100F (2012) and EU RAR (ECHA, 2000) – summaries of several radiolabel studies.

2. Weight-of-evidence outcome
   Primary routes of excretion (descending order of importance):
   a) Exhalation – unchanged parent VC.  
   b) Exhalation – 14CO2 generated after oxidation and complete metabolism.  
   c) Urine – water-soluble mercapturic acids, chiefly thiodiglycolic (thiodiacetic) acid (TDAA) and 2-chloroacetic acid.  
   d) Faeces – only trace amounts (< 2 % of dose).

3. Quantitative pattern (typical radiolabel studies, 48-h cumulative recovery)
   • 55-65 % of administered 14C recovered as CO2 in expired air.  
   • 5-15 % recovered as unchanged VC in expired air (higher fraction immediately after exposure).  
   • 20-30 % recovered in urine as polar metabolites (TDAA accounts for > 70 % of urinary radioactivity).  
   • < 2 % in faeces; ~2-3 % retained in tissues at 48 h (mainly liver).  
   Total recovery generally 90-95 %.

4. Elimination kinetics
   Parent compound  
   • Extremely rapid blood clearance because of high volatility and low blood:air partition ratio (~2).  
   • After cessation of inhalation (200-500 ppm), arterial VC concentrations in rats decline mono-exponentially with a half-life (t½) of 1–3 min (Bolt et al., 1976).  
   • > 90 % of inhaled parent VC is removed from the body within the first 30 min post-exposure by pulmonary exhalation.

   Total radioactivity (metabolites + CO2)  
   • Biphasic elimination: fast phase (t½ ≈ 1 h; mainly CO2) followed by a slower phase (t½ ≈ 5–7 h; urinary metabolites).  
   • ~75 % of the total 14C dose is excreted within 6 h; > 90 % within 24 h; excretion essentially complete (≥ 95 %) by 48 h.  
   • No evidence for long-term accumulation; tissue residues fall below 1 % of dose after 72 h.

5. Influence of dose and route
   • At high inhalation concentrations (> 1 000 ppm) metabolic pathways (CYP2E1) saturate; the proportion of unchanged VC in expired air rises while urinary metabolite fraction falls.  
   • After i.v. or oral administration the overall pattern is similar, but unchanged VC in breath is lower (≤ 3 %) and urinary metabolites predominate (≈ 45 % of dose).

6. Uncertainties / Data gaps
   • Limited published information on female rats and on very low-dose (< 1 ppm) exposures.  
   • Metabolite profiling in bile is scarce; minor biliary excretion cannot be ruled out.  
   • Strain differences (e.g., Fischer-344 vs Wistar) have not been systematically compared, but available data indicate similar qualitative patterns.

7. Expert judgement
   • Systemic availability: High – VC is almost completely absorbed via the lungs, but extensive first-pass metabolism limits persistence of the parent compound.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High – reactive epoxide intermediates form DNA adducts; however, these aspects are outside the present question on excretion.

Conclusion
In rats, vinyl chloride is eliminated rapidly. Soon after exposure, the parent compound is cleared almost entirely through the lungs (t½ ≈ 1–3 min). Over the subsequent hours, oxidative metabolism converts most of the absorbed dose to CO2 (expired) or to polar sulfur-containing acids that are excreted in urine. By 24 h > 90 % of the administered dose has left the body, and by 48 h elimination is essentially complete, with only trace residues remaining in tissues. Faecal and other routes play a negligible role in the overall clearance of vinyl chloride in the rat.
